draft.introduction: Al-Fatiha ("The Opening") is the first chapter of the Quran and the most recited passage in Islam, repeated in every cycle of daily prayer. Its seven verses encapsulate the entire theology of the Quran: divine sovereignty, mercy, judgment, and the human plea for guidance.

translation.text: In the name of God, the Most Compassionate, the Most Merciful. All praise belongs to God, Lord of all the worlds. The Most Compassionate, the Most Merciful. Master of the Day of Judgment.
translation.original_text: بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمَـنِ الرَّحِيمِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ الرَّحْمَـنِ الرَّحِيمِ مَالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ

translation.text: You alone we worship, and You alone we ask for help. Guide us along the straight path, the path of those You have blessed—not those who have earned Your anger, nor those who have gone astray.
translation.original_text: إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وإِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ اهْدِنَا الصِّرَاطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ

translator_notes.note: The basmala ("In the name of God") prefaces every surah except the ninth. The paired divine names al-Rahman and al-Rahim both derive from the root r-h-m (womb, mercy), emphasizing that compassion is the primary attribute through which God relates to creation.
